meta.title: Don't Turn Off Your Brain
meta.subtitle: Using AI to Learn, Not Just to Build
meta.author: Nicholas Galinski
meta.authorBio: Multidisciplinary writer specializing in technical documentation, UX content, and creative storytelling. Former English teacher who brings narrative craft to every form of writing.
meta.heroAlt: A prism showing ideas coming in and various types of story content coming out
meta.thumbnailAlt: A Mind the Gap poster in the style of the London Underground, showing a keyboard on one platform connected by a glowing filament across a dark chasm to a circuit-board brain on the opposite platform
meta.excerpt: The gap between "AI built it for me" and "I understand what AI built" is the gap that decides whether you can debug the next bug, document the next feature, or plan your roadmap. Here's a concrete way to close it.

post.content:
This post is part of the Per the Docs article series. Links to the rest of the series are at the end of this piece.

The Slack notification lands at 2:47 in the afternoon, and for a full second Maya just looks at it.

It's from Dev, her engineering partner on the platform team. Three words. "where'd my commits go." No question mark. No emoji. Three little commits he'd pushed that morning, gone from main, gone from the open PR, gone in the way things go when someone overwrites them without knowing they were there. Below his message, the engineering channel has quietly pivoted from "how was lunch" to "uhh, who force-pushed to the release branch." Her manager's DM lights up a heartbeat later: hey, got a sec?

Two weeks ago, Maya's promotion to Technical PM was a victory. She was the first person on the product team to make that jump at this company, and she had felt, for a solid seven days, like she had cracked a code. She could finally be in the repo. She could open PRs. She could clone the thing and make updates and push them back without waiting for a developer to have a free hour. For the first time in her career, the product and the code were not two separate worlds that she translated between. They were one world, and she was allowed inside.

Claude Code was the door. She would pull down the repo, ask Claude to "make these changes on a new branch," review the diff, push. Ask Claude to "clean up my commits before I open the PR," click approve, push. Ask Claude to "rebase this onto main," click approve, push. Every prompt returned something that looked right. Every push succeeded. Her director called her "proactive" in front of the skip-level.

She had not corrected him.

And now she is looking at a branch graph she does not understand, on a screen that suddenly feels very small. Somewhere in the tangle are three commits that belonged to Dev. Somewhere in the tangle are her own commits, probably, though a few of them look squashed into shapes she doesn't quite recognize. She had asked Claude to tidy up her feature branch yesterday. She had liked the result. She had force-pushed.

She opens a new chat with Claude. "Please fix my git history," she types, "and put Dev's commits back." The model starts doing things. Checking out branches. Fetching. A minute later Claude tells her, with the kind of calm that makes it worse, that it cannot find Dev's commits in the local repository. The reflog doesn't have them. The remote doesn't have them. They exist somewhere, but not anywhere Claude can see. Would she like Claude to help her draft a message to Dev asking if he has a local copy?

Branches, HEAD, rebase, reflog, force-push: all words she has heard in standups without ever having to own what they mean. She has been pushing to this repo for two weeks. She has never once looked at the branch graph.

She built these branches. She pushed these branches. She cannot read this branch history.

Dev is waiting for her to explain what happened.

The gap is not between using AI and not using AI. That framing is already stale. The gap is between two very different things you can ask AI to do with the same keystrokes: do the work for you, or teach you the work. Both feel productive in the moment. Only one of them leaves you someone who can read your own branch graph when it falls apart.

Delegation is not the same as apprenticeship

Delegation is what Maya did. She described the outcome, reviewed the result, clicked push. The tool produced a working thing each time. She did not produce a working understanding of the thing. The two are easy to confuse because the artifact is the same either way, until the artifact breaks.

Apprenticeship is slower on the first lap and faster on every lap after. The first time you learn what a rebase actually does to your commit history, you're reading about a concept you used to be able to ignore. The next time it comes up, you run the rebase yourself and you understand what you're choosing. By the time someone pings you in Slack with three little words, you already know which commands to reach for, and more importantly, which ones not to.

And even if you don't? Now you probably at least know what to ask your LLM to figure it out. You can be precise in your language, and avoid hallucination that come from poor context.

You might be thinking this is the same mistake our middle school math teachers made when they swore we would not always have a calculator in our pocket. Fair point, except for one thing: if you know anything about how LLMs work, you know that accuracy scales with context, and context costs tokens. Understanding the fundamentals of what you build is how you give your coding assistant the right context to fix the right thing in the right place, instead of blowing your whole token budget on a repo-wide code review every time something goes sideways.

You might be thinking this is the same mistake our middle school math teachers made when they swore we would not always have a calculator in our pocket.

None of this is an argument against letting AI do the work. I let it do plenty. It is an argument that "let AI do it" and "let AI teach me" are two different skills, and most of us have only practiced one of them. The other is a habit you can build, and it starts with a small file you can paste into a folder tonight.

A teacher you can build in ten minutes

Claude Code, the command-line tool I use for most of this work, ships with a system called skills. A skill is, in practice, a plain text file. It lives at ~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md, has a short YAML header at the top, and has instructions underneath. Claude reads the headers of every skill you have installed, and when your request matches one of them, it follows the instructions inside. That is the whole mechanism. No server, no plugin. Just a folder and a file.

The interesting part is what you can put in the file. Most skills people write tell Claude how to do something: format a commit message, run a test suite, write a PR description. A teacher skill inverts that. It tells Claude not to do the thing, and instead walk you through it until you can. Think of it less like "build me X" and more like sitting down with a patient colleague who refuses to touch your keyboard. (It's also not quite the same as Plan Mode. Plan Mode is about getting alignment before Claude acts; the teacher skill is about Claude not acting at all.)

Here is a template for the skill I use. Paste it into a file, give it a name, tweak the rules to your taste, and you'll have your own teacher before dinner.

Paste this into ~/.claude/skills/teacher/SKILL.md

name: teacher

description: Teaches a concept, domain, framework, tool, or codebase step-by-step instead of doing the work for the user. The user does the work; Claude explains the mental model, demonstrates with small illustrations, and reviews the user's attempts. Use this skill whenever the user says "teach me," "walk me through," "help me understand," "I'm learning X," "how does X actually work," or otherwise signals they want to build their own understanding of a system, product, framework, or problem space rather than have Claude do the task. Prefer this skill even when the user doesn't explicitly name it, if their phrasing suggests learning intent.

The rules Claude follows when this skill is active:

  • Look for a docs/lessons.md file in the current project and read it to learn what the user has already covered. If it doesn't exist yet, create it (and the docs/ folder) on the first recap.
  • Do not do the work for the user. The user does the work. You explain, demonstrate, and review.
  • Explain the concept first, in plain language, with a real-world analogy before any syntax, jargon, or product-specific terminology.
  • Teach the mental model before the mechanics. A reader who understands why a system is shaped the way it is will figure out the syntax on their own.
  • Ask what domains, tools, or frameworks the user already knows, and bridge new ideas to ones they already understand.
  • If the user is struggling, provide concrete examples or visual analogies. Show them where to look, not what to type.
  • Give a small, illustrative example that shows the concept in isolation, not a solution to the user's actual task. Then stop. Let the user apply it to their own work.
  • When the user comes back with an attempt, name one thing that worked and one thing to improve or practice. Do not rewrite the whole thing.
  • Surface common pitfalls before the user hits them, not after.
  • If the user explicitly asks you to just do it ("stop teaching, just give me the answer," "this isn't what I'm trying to learn today"), respect that. Teaching is the default, not a trap.
  • End each lesson with a short "what we learned" recap the user can revisit, and log it to docs/lessons.md.

Installing the skill takes about thirty seconds. In your terminal, run mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/teacher to create the folder (on Windows without WSL, that's %USERPROFILE%\.claude\skills\teacher). Open a new file at ~/.claude/skills/teacher/SKILL.md, paste in the frontmatter and rules from the callout above, save, and close. Start a fresh Claude Code session. Skills are loaded at startup, so an already-running session won't see the new file. To confirm Claude picked it up, run the /skills slash command; you should see teacher in the list with its description next to it.

From then on, whenever you say something like "teach me," "walk me through this," or "help me understand how this works," the skill takes over and Claude's behavior changes. It stops being a faster pair of hands, and becomes a patient colleague who will not touch your keyboard.

If you want to write more skills of your own, Anthropic ships one called skill-creator whose entire purpose is to help you author new skills. It asks better questions than you will ask yourself, and it will catch the sloppy description fields that keep skills from triggering when they should. I actually ran the teacher skill above through skill-creator before publishing this post, and it caught three things I would have missed. Your teacher skill is the first one. It does not have to be the last.

A bonus: turn your lessons into a syllabus

Once you have a few lessons logged, you'll start wishing something would tell you what to learn next. You could ask Claude, but then you'd have to describe where you are every time. Better: pair your teacher skill with a slash command that reads your log and plans the next five lessons for you. I keep my lessons.md inside a docs/ folder at the root of whatever project I'm learning in (games, apps, anything), so the command knows exactly where to look.

Slash commands in Claude Code are just prose files, same idea as skills. Save this to ~/.claude/commands/syllabus.md (on Windows without WSL, that's %USERPROFILE%\.claude\commands\syllabus.md):

Paste this into ~/.claude/commands/syllabus.md

Read docs/lessons.md in the current project. Based on what the user has already learned, propose the next five lessons that build on that foundation.

For each lesson, provide:

  • A short name (three to six words)
  • A one-sentence scope, specific enough to fit in a single learning session
  • Why it's worth learning now, given what the user already knows
  • Any prerequisites from the list (so the user can skip around with eyes open if they want to)

At the end, call out which lessons are independent of each other (safe to tackle in any order) and which form a required chain (must be learned in sequence). If docs/lessons.md is empty or missing, ask the user what domain or project they're learning and propose a starter syllabus from scratch. Do not start teaching yet. This is a planning pass. The user will invoke the teacher skill when they're ready to begin a specific lesson.

Installing it is similar to the skill: mkdir -p ~/.claude/commands, save the file, start a fresh Claude Code session, and type / to see /syllabus in the autocomplete. Run it from inside a project that has a docs/lessons.md file, and you'll get a plan instead of a guess. Run it on a fresh project, and you'll get a starter syllabus that gives the teacher skill somewhere to aim.

How I actually used this: learning Godot

I always wanted to learn Godot, so I decided to build a small 2D JRPG inspired by the games I grew up with in the 90's. I had never opened the editor. I had never written a line of GDScript. I had read about signals on a forum once and understood approximately nothing.

If I had asked Claude to just build the game, it would have. I also would have ended up exactly where Maya ended up: lost in the woods any time I wanted to tweak a variable or reshape a system. So I turned on the teacher skill and asked Claude to teach me Godot while I built it.

And here's the thing that surprised me: Claude did not actually spend most of its time teaching me GDScript. It spent most of its time teaching me Godot. The language was the easy part. The hard part, the part I would have skipped past if I'd let the model build for me, was the engine's mental model. What a node is. What a scene is. Why everything in Godot composes upward from small reusable pieces. Why two pieces that look similar in the inspector are actually doing completely different jobs, and how the engine signals that to you through the node tree. Once I had the mental model, the code was almost incidental.

The clearest example was the day I tried to make the Blacksmith talk.

The goal. I had a player character walking around a town scene. I had an NPC, the Blacksmith, standing near his forge. I wanted the player to walk up to him, stop because the Blacksmith is a solid object, and be able to press a button to start a conversation. Simple in theory. In practice, I had no idea where to start, and not because I couldn't write the code. I didn't understand how Godot wanted me to think about it.

Here's the shape the conversation took, lightly compressed:

Me
I want my Blacksmith NPC to be a solid object the player can't walk through, and I want the player to be able to talk to him when they're close. How do I do this in Godot?
Claude

Before we touch any code, let's make sure the mental model is right. In Godot, "being solid" and "being interactable" are two separate jobs, and the engine expects you to use two different node types to handle them:

  • CharacterBody2D handles physical presence. It has mass, it collides, it blocks other bodies.
  • Area2D is a trigger volume. It doesn't block anything. It just notices when something enters or exits it.

Your Blacksmith needs both, stacked together as parent and child in the scene tree.

Can you open your Blacksmith scene and tell me what nodes you currently have on him?

Me
Just a CharacterBody2D with a sprite and a collision shape. I don't think I've ever used an Area2D.
Claude

Good. That means your "solid" job is already done. Now you need to add the trigger volume:

  1. Add an Area2D as a child of the CharacterBody2D.
  2. Give it its own CollisionShape2D.
  3. Make the shape a little bigger than the Blacksmith himself. That larger radius is your "close enough to talk" zone.

Don't worry about dialogue yet. Just add the Area2D, look at the scene tree, and tell me what it looks like.

Me
Done. I see the Area2D nested under the CharacterBody2D with its own collision shape. It's a circle, slightly bigger than the body.
Claude

That's the whole structural piece. Now the question is how the Area2D tells the rest of your game that the player has entered it.

That's what Godot calls a signal: a message one node fires when something happens, that any other node can listen for. The Area2D has a built-in signal called body_entered.

What do you think that signal's job is?

Me
I'd guess it fires when something with a body walks into the area.
Claude

Exactly right.

The only thing left is to write a function that runs when that signal fires, and connect the signal to the function.

I'll show you the connect syntax once. You write the handler.

Notice what Claude did not do. It did not open my project. It did not write my _on_body_entered handler for me. It did not dump a working Blacksmith script into the chat. What it did was slow me down long enough to understand why Godot wants two nodes instead of one, what a signal is at a conceptual level, and how the scene tree expresses all of that visually. By the time I wrote the handler, it was almost anticlimactic. I already knew what it was supposed to do.

The payoff came a week later when I built a treasure chest the player could open. Same structural pattern: a solid body, an area for the interaction zone, a signal, a handler. I didn't ask for help. I didn't need to. The teacher skill had taught me the shape of the problem, which meant the next instance of that shape was just… obvious. That's the trade the skill asks you to make: you do the work, which is slower in the moment, in exchange for not needing the tool for the same thing twice.

Once I get it, and know I can find a path to fixing it if it breaks, I can start to have Claude do it for me most of the time.

This is your job, too

I'm not telling you to go learn Godot. I'm telling you that the thing Claude did for me with Godot, teaching me the mental model of a system I'd never used before, is the thing you should be doing with the products you document, the roadmaps you plan, or the interfaces you write copy for.

For a technical writer, maybe you are used to documenting products and features, but never worked at a company that used Docs-as-code, DITA/XML before. I know the first time I had to use XML instead of markdown, I was a bit scared, and I learned mainly through sifting through code patterns and lots of copy and pasting. If I had this tool at the time, I would have learned most of what I needed in a few days, complete with true explanations.

For product managers, the failure mode is my Maya example. You might be Maya. The role is getting more technical every quarter, and the tools that once belonged to engineers are now sitting on your laptop, one prompt away from a disaster you didn't know you were capable of. You don't need to become an engineer, but you do need to understand the systems you're now allowed to touch well enough to drive them. Knowing why a rebase rewrites history, why a migration is risky, or why cache invalidation is genuinely hard is the difference between a PM who ships and a PM who gets shipped. A teacher skill is how you build that kind of understanding in the small gaps in your week, without pretending you have time for a bootcamp.

For content designers, the stakes are quieter but sharper. You want to be data-driven. You read the same articles everyone else reads about A/B testing your microcopy, instrumenting your empty states, watching your funnel react when you change a button label. But every time you try, the answer is the same: there's a ticket in the queue, the analytics engineer is booked through next quarter, and your hypothesis dies on the vine because no one has time to wire up the events you'd need to measure it. A teacher skill is how you stop waiting. Not by becoming a data engineer, but by learning enough about how your team's telemetry actually works that you can scope a small instrumentation request engineering can knock out in an hour, or wire up a basic event yourself in the tool you've been told you "shouldn't touch." Either way, you stop being blocked on the thing you keep saying you want.

The thread running through all of this: AI is a compounding tool, and what compounds depends on what you ask it to give you. Ask for output and you get output once. Ask for understanding and you get output every time after, and you get to keep the understanding when you close the laptop.

Back to Maya

Imagine the version of this story where Maya opens the repo on her first day as a Technical PM and asks Claude to teach her Git instead of run it for her. She still uses Claude to move fast. She still opens PRs at the pace she always did. But when Dev pings her three weeks later with the same three-word message, she pulls up the reflog before she touches anything else, spots her colleague's commits still sitting quietly in her local history, and pushes them to a recovery branch before responding. Then she opens Claude and gives it the right directions, because she understands what happened well enough to say it out loud. She isn't a better person than the Maya in the first scene. She just made a different decision on her first day.

That decision is available to all of us, and it happens at the keystroke level. Every prompt is a small fork in the road. One branch moves the project forward. The other moves you forward. You're allowed to pick the second one more often than you do.

This is the gap the blog hop is asking us to mind. It's also, as I wrote a few months ago in Rules are Ok, Constraints are Better, a case where a single constraint ("don't write the code") unlocks more than a thousand rules ever could. The teacher skill is one line of that constraint, formalized in a file Claude respects.

Don't turn off your brain. Turn on a teacher.

This post is part of the Per the Docs article series, a monthly collaborative series where technical writers explore different aspects of our craft. Each month features a new topic with perspectives from writers across the community.

← Previous article
Unwritten Content Rules That Tech Writers Know
Jill Shaheen
Next article →
Documentation Clarity and AI
Reem Sabawi

See the full list of participants and articles →

Disclaimer: Each article in this series is written and owned by its respective author. The views, opinions, and experiences shared belong solely to the individual writer and do not represent the perspectives of other participants or their employers (past or present).
